title: Trade Like a Grown-Up – Discipline,
content: Trade Like a Grown-Up – Discipline, Defence, and a Bit of Devilry
I. Framing the Game – Trading Is Not Gambling (Unless You Are)

Trading is deceptively simple: buy low, sell high. Or short high, buy back lower. Easy to say, fatal to underestimate. The market is a game of probabilities wrapped in uncertainty, powered by emotion.
“A winning system with a losing mindset is a losing system.” – Dr. David Paul
Key Takeaway:
You are not here to be right. You are here to survive long enough to be right enough. Your weapon is process. Your shield is risk management. Your undoing is usually ego.
II. Money Management – Ammunition Control
Position Sizing
● Never risk more than 1–2% of total capital on a single position. ● Think in terms of risk units, not trade size. A big position on a strong setup is still valid—if
it fits your risk unit.
DCA (Dollar-Cost Averaging) In and Out
● Don’t blow the powder all at once. Enter in measured clips. Let the market confirm your
view.
● Exit the same way. Scale out methodically. Lock in gains while leaving room to run.
raw_text:
Trade Like a Grown-Up – Discipline, 
Defence, and a Bit of Devilry 

I. Framing the Game – Trading Is Not Gambling (Unless 
You Are) 

Trading is deceptively simple: buy low, sell high. Or short high, buy back lower. Easy to say, fatal 
to underestimate. The market is a game of probabilities wrapped in uncertainty, powered by 
emotion. 

“A winning system with a losing mindset is a losing system.” – Dr. David Paul 

Key Takeaway: 

You are not here to be right. You are here to survive long enough to be right enough. Your 
weapon is process. Your shield is risk management. Your undoing is usually ego. 

II. Money Management – Ammunition Control 

Position Sizing 

●  Never risk more than 1–2% of total capital on a single position. 
●  Think in terms of risk units, not trade size. A big position on a strong setup is still valid—if 

it fits your risk unit. 

DCA (Dollar-Cost Averaging) In and Out 

●  Don’t blow the powder all at once. Enter in measured clips. Let the market confirm your 

view. 

●  Exit the same way. Scale out methodically. Lock in gains while leaving room to run.

title: The Danger of Clustering
content: The Danger of Clustering
Illustration: Picture 5 trades in a row. Each one risks 3%. Three losses = -9%. Two more losses? -15%. You’re rattled, emotional, and now trading to “get it back.”
● Clustering is when multiple losses (or wins) occur in quick succession. ● ● The solution is proper risk limits and awareness of market regime. Reduce size or step
In a high-cluster environment, even a good system can suffer.
back when variance spikes.
Rule of Thumb: When clustered losses appear, assume the market has changed—or you have. Stop. Reassess.
III. Risk Management – The Art of Not Dying
The Line in the Sand
● Every trade should have a clear invalidation point. ●
It’s not an emotional threshold—it’s a technical or fundamental “this-no-longer-makes-sense” point.
● This is where hedging, not panicking, comes in.
Hedging – Your Style
Instead of hard stops, use counter trades to balance risk.
● ● Particularly useful in macro positions (e.g. Natural Gas). When the setup is long but price
is tanking, short a correlated or inverse position to neutralise exposure.
Avoid Overleveraging
● Leverage magnifies your skill—and your stupidity. ● Size kills. Trade small enough to be wrong several times in a row and still be in the
game.
Scenario Planning:
Have a plan for multiple market moods: trending, chopping, breaking news, liquidity events.

title: IV. Trading Psychology – The Inner Arena
content: IV. Trading Psychology – The Inner Arena
Ego Is Not Your Edge
● Overconfidence leads to size increases right before a regime change. ● Humility keeps you safe. Confidence in process, not prediction.
Emotional Mastery
● Fight/Flight leads to revenge trades, overtrading, and stubbornness. ● Cultivate mindfulness. Observe yourself as you trade. Use journaling.
Be the Analyst of the Trader
The trader is reactive. The analyst is reflective. Be both.
● Review every trade. ● Note not just price action, but emotional action: fear, hesitation, greed.
V. Process > Prediction
Setups, Not Wishes
● Only enter trades with clear technical or fundamental rationale. ● Avoid impulsive trades based on headlines or FOMO.
Journaling
● Record setup, entry, exit, rationale, and emotional state. ● Review monthly. Look for patterns in behaviour.
No Trade = Position
● Sitting out is an active decision. ● Sideways markets, unclear trends, or mental fatigue = cash is a position.
raw_text:
IV. Trading Psychology – The Inner Arena 

Ego Is Not Your Edge 

●  Overconfidence leads to size increases right before a regime change. 
●  Humility keeps you safe. Confidence in process, not prediction. 

Emotional Mastery 

●  Fight/Flight leads to revenge trades, overtrading, and stubbornness. 
●  Cultivate mindfulness. Observe yourself as you trade. Use journaling. 

Be the Analyst of the Trader 

The trader is reactive. The analyst is reflective. Be both. 

●  Review every trade. 
●  Note not just price action, but emotional action: fear, hesitation, greed. 

V. Process > Prediction 

Setups, Not Wishes 

●  Only enter trades with clear technical or fundamental rationale. 
●  Avoid impulsive trades based on headlines or FOMO. 

Journaling 

●  Record setup, entry, exit, rationale, and emotional state. 
●  Review monthly. Look for patterns in behaviour. 

No Trade = Position 

●  Sitting out is an active decision. 
●  Sideways markets, unclear trends, or mental fatigue = cash is a position.

title: VI. Building Edge – Brick by Brick
content: VI. Building Edge – Brick by Brick
Layered Confluence
● Combine market structure, dynamics, macro news, and price action. ● The more aligned the signals, the more confident the trade.
Ritual and Routine
● Pre-market scan. Key level mapping. ● Daily brief (your own or in group). ● Trade review, even if no trades.
Trade Community
● Solitary trading leads to blind spots. ● Use your Discord room: test ideas, vent losses, share setups. Iron sharpens iron.
VII. Final Punch – The Trader’s Code
● Stay alive. That’s the first job. ● Be boring in process, brilliant in execution. ● Size small, think big. Never fall in love with a trade.
“Consistency isn’t sexy. But neither is losing your house.”
raw_text:
VI. Building Edge – Brick by Brick 

Layered Confluence 

●  Combine market structure, dynamics, macro news, and price action. 
●  The more aligned the signals, the more confident the trade. 

Ritual and Routine 

●  Pre-market scan. Key level mapping. 
●  Daily brief (your own or in group). 
●  Trade review, even if no trades. 

Trade Community 

●  Solitary trading leads to blind spots. 
●  Use your Discord room: test ideas, vent losses, share setups. Iron sharpens iron. 

VII. Final Punch – The Trader’s Code 

●  Stay alive. That’s the first job. 
●  Be boring in process, brilliant in execution. 
●  Size small, think big. Never fall in love with a trade. 

“Consistency isn’t sexy. But neither is losing your house.”
